name: (-)-syringaresnol-4-O-D-apiofuranosyl-(1→2)-D-glucopyranoside
contraindications: may potentiate sedative or antidepressant drugs
avoid in pregnancy or lactation unless supervised.

name: 5-MeO-DMT
description: 5-MeO-DMT is a short-acting psychedelic tryptamine found in some plants and toad secretions, with intense and rapidly onset alterations in perception and self-processing.
mechanism: Primary mechanism is serotonergic receptor agonism, especially strong 5-HT1A activity with additional 5-HT2A-mediated psychedelic effects.
safetyNotes: Explicit risks include panic, severe confusion, accidents, emesis/aspiration risk, and dangerous behavior during acute intoxication. Interaction categories: serotonergic drugs (SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs/triptans; serotonin-toxicity risk), sympathomimetics/stimulants (cardiovascular stress), and other psychoactives/CNS depressants that impair judgment.

name: 7-hydroxymitragynine
description: 7-Hydroxymitragynine is a kratom alkaloid present at low natural levels but with high opioid-receptor potency relative to mitragynine.
mechanism: Acts primarily as a μ-opioid receptor partial agonist and drives opioid-like analgesic and respiratory-depressant effects.
safetyNotes: Key risks include sedation, respiratory depression, dependence, and withdrawal with repeated exposure. Interaction categories: other CNS depressants (opioids, benzodiazepines, alcohol, gabapentinoids), sedating antihistamines, and additional opioid agonists with additive overdose risk.

name: 7α-hydroxybaruol
effects: Traditional uses include skin and respiratory conditions
contraindications: high doses may cause gastrointestinal upset or phototoxic reactions due to hydroquinone.

name: acetylcholine
description: Acetylcholine is a core human neurotransmitter released at muscarinic and nicotinic synapses, including autonomic ganglia, parasympathetic targets, and the neuromuscular junction.
mechanism: Activates muscarinic and nicotinic acetylcholine receptors; signaling is rapidly terminated by acetylcholinesterase hydrolysis in the synaptic cleft.
safetyNotes: Acetylcholine excess produces cholinergic toxicity patterns (bradycardia, bronchospasm, diarrhea, sweating, salivation, muscle weakness). Interaction categories: cholinergic agonists/acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (additive cholinergic effects) and anticholinergics (pharmacologic antagonism).

name: Quercetin

sources.title: PubMed review literature on quercetin

description: Quercetin is a dietary flavonol studied for antioxidant and inflammatory-pathway modulation, with early human data but limited high-certainty outcomes for major indications.
mechanism: Influences NF-kB-linked inflammatory signaling and may reduce mast-cell mediator release in preclinical and early translational work.
safetyNotes: Food exposure is generally safe; high-dose supplements may affect drug transport/metabolism. Caution with anticoagulants, immunosuppressants, and kidney disease at high doses.
summary: Flavonol widely distributed in herbs and fruits, commonly linked to NF-κB, PI3K/Akt, and Nrf2 pathway modulation.

name: Magnesium
contraindications: High supplemental doses can cause GI effects

sources.title: Magnesium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)

description: Magnesium is an essential mineral cofactor for hundreds of enzymes involved in ATP handling, neuromuscular function, and electrolyte-dependent cellular signaling.
mechanism: Stabilizes ATP-dependent reactions and modulates ion-channel excitability in nerve and muscle tissue.
safetyNotes: Excess supplemental magnesium can cause diarrhea; renal impairment increases risk of accumulation and should prompt medical supervision.

name: Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6)
contraindications: Avoid prolonged high-dose self-supplementation due to neuropathy risk
Use medication review when combined with anticonvulsants or levodopa regimens

sources.title: Vitamin B6 Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)

name: Riboflavin (Vitamin B2)
effects: Supports energy metabolism via FMN/FAD cofactors
contraindications: No major toxicity concerns at typical use levels
High-dose use for migraine prevention differs from nutritional dosing

sources.title: Vitamin B2 Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)
NIH/NCCIH migraine prophylaxis guidance references

description: Riboflavin (vitamin B2) is an essential water-soluble micronutrient that forms FMN and FAD cofactors for cellular energy metabolism and antioxidant enzyme activity.
mechanism: Converted to FMN/FAD cofactors that support mitochondrial redox reactions and glutathione recycling pathways.
safetyNotes: Generally safe at nutritional doses; high supplemental doses can cause bright-yellow urine and occasional mild GI discomfort.

name: Selenium
contraindications: Avoid chronic intake above upper limit
Non-deficiency high-dose use may add risk without clear benefit

sources.title: Selenium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)

name: Vitamin D (Calciferols)
effects: Prevents vitamin D deficiency bone disease
contraindications: Avoid chronic high-dose unsupervised use because of hypercalcemia risk

sources.title: Vitamin D Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)
Institute of Medicine vitamin D report (2011)

name: L-Tryptophan
effects: Supports serotonin and melatonin precursor pathways
contraindications: EMS history linked to contaminated products; purity matters

sources.title: Nutrients Involved in Serotonin and Melatonin Synthesis (NIH/PMC article)

name: Melatonin

sources.title: Melatonin: What You Need To Know (NCCIH)
PubMed review literature on melatonin indications

description: Melatonin is an endogenous indoleamine hormone used as a supplement to support circadian phase alignment, sleep-onset timing, and jet-lag symptom reduction in selected settings.
mechanism: Acts primarily at MT1 and MT2 receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus to shift circadian timing signals.
safetyNotes: May cause next-day drowsiness in some users; review interactions with sedatives and anticoagulants before routine use.

name: Niacin (Vitamin B3)
effects: At pharmacologic doses can lower triglycerides and raise HDL
contraindications: High-dose use requires medical supervision because hepatotoxicity risk is formulation-dependent
Flushing and vasodilatory adverse effects are common with immediate-release pharmacologic dosing

sources.title: Niacin Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)
FDA niacin safety communications (formulation-dependent risk context)

description: Niacin (vitamin B3) is a precursor to NAD/NADP coenzymes central to energy transfer, and pharmacologic doses are used clinically for lipid effects under medical supervision.
mechanism: Supports NAD/NADP-dependent redox metabolism; high-dose nicotinic acid also activates GPR109A signaling relevant to lipid modulation.
safetyNotes: Pharmacologic doses can cause flushing and hepatotoxicity risk; use high-dose protocols only with clinician monitoring.

name: Omega-3 Fatty Acids (EPA/DHA)
effects: Supports triglyceride reduction at higher doses
Supports membrane and neural lipid biology
contraindications: Use caution with bleeding-risk conditions or concurrent anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy
Supplement quality/purity and oxidation status vary across products

sources.title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)
FDA prescription omega-3 product labeling context

mechanism: Compete with arachidonic-acid pathways and generate specialized pro-resolving mediators that shift inflammatory signaling tone.
description: EPA and DHA are long-chain omega-3 fatty acids that contribute to membrane signaling, inflammation-resolution pathways, and cardiovascular-risk modification in selected populations.
safetyNotes: May increase bleeding tendency at higher doses or with anticoagulants; product quality and oxidation control are important.

name: Probiotics
effects: May reduce antibiotic-associated diarrhea in some strains
May modestly improve some IBS symptoms (strain dependent)
contraindications: Use caution in severely immunocompromised/ICU patients
Products are not interchangeable across strains

sources.title: Probiotics (MedlinePlus)
Probiotics Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)

name: Resveratrol
contraindications: caution with anticoagulants/antiplatelets, hormone-sensitive conditions, pregnancy, and high-dose extracts.

sources.title: PubMed review literature on resveratrol

description: Stilbene best known from knotweed and grape sources, strongly linked to SIRT1, AMPK, and NF-κB pathways.
summary: Stilbene best known from knotweed and grape sources, strongly linked to SIRT1, AMPK, and NF-κB pathways.
safetyNotes: Usually well tolerated short-term; caution with anticoagulants/antiplatelets, hormone-sensitive conditions, pregnancy, and high-dose extracts.

name: S-Adenosyl-L-Methionine (SAMe)
effects: May support depressive symptom improvement in some studies
May reduce osteoarthritis pain in selected trials
Acts as a biological methyl donor in endogenous pathways
contraindications: Can precipitate mania/hypomania in bipolar-spectrum patients
Use caution with serotonergic psychiatric medications due to serotonin-syndrome concern

sources.title: SAMe: In Depth (NCCIH/NIH)
PubMed clinical review literature on SAMe for depression and osteoarthritis
